title: ASIC slams super safety failures
full_text:
The corporate regulator is warning super trustees that they are failing to adequately protect member savings from a repeat of the Shield and First Guardian fund collapses.
After a review into super platforms growing in popularity, ASIC found “stark and persistent failures to protect retirement savings”, due to a lack of monitoring wanton fees, harmful deductions, and high-risk strategies.
In compiling its report, ASIC reviewed six platform trustees responsible for more than $300bn in savings over a 17-month period from June 2024 to October 2025.
In that time, $2.6bn was paid in advice fees by 720,000 advised members. Only 2580 checks of advice documents were reported, with 250 adverse findings.
One of the trustees performed only 21 checks during the 17-month period, with 75 per cent of those checks resulting in adverse findings.
ASIC commissioner Simone Constant said it was “clear” trustees had not learned the harsh lessons from Shield and First Guardian.
“In one disturbing case, a trustee failed to take further action for 13 months after becoming aware of suspicious activity from a representative of an advice licensee,” Ms Constant said.
“Many of the clear gaps in oversight are deeply concerning and difficult to justify. Trustees should not expose their members’ retirement savings to unacceptable risks in the pursuit of volume growth.” ASIC said advice fee controls had “persistent gaps”. One trustee aimed to increase the advice fee cap to $30,000. The highest advice fee paid during the period was $25,000. Three of the six trustees did not have an upper limit set, and percentage-based fee caps ranged from 1.7 per cent to 10 per cent.
The review also found half of reporting trustees did not conduct any checks of advice documents for at least one of the 17 months; there was “insufficient focus” on whether licensee business models used lead generators or third-party referrals; and “inadequate monitoring of key risk indicators” such as unusual fund flows or holding limits.
Ms Constant warned trustees to immediately consider how they can improve protections. “Where we identify significant noncompliance, we will not hesitate to exercise our regulatory powers, including enforcement action,” she said.
